query: What are all plant stress indicators the AI agent should monitor? Include nutrient deficiency symptoms, disease indicators, environmental stress signs, and water stress markers for each crop type.

content.text: {"statusCode":200,"body":"{\"retrieved_chunks\": [{\"content\": \"# 4. Plant Stress and Response Guide for Autonomous Greenhouse Systems\\n## 4.1 Purpose\\nThis document describes major abiotic stressors relevant to a Martian controlled-environment greenhouse, including:\\n* Physiological mechanisms\\n* Observable symptoms\\n* Impact on crop yield and nutritional value\\n* Mitigation strategies within a controlled agriculture system\\nThe objective is to support AI agents in:\\n* Detecting plant stress conditions\\n* Diagnosing probable causes\\n* Triggering corrective environmental or nutritional actions\\n* Optimizing long-term crop stability and yield\\n---\\n# 4.2 Overview of Abiotic Stress in Controlled Environments\\nAbiotic stress refers to negative impacts on plant growth caused by non-living environmental factors.\\nPrimary stress categories relevant to Mars greenhouse systems:\\n1. Water Stress (Drought or Overwatering)\\n2. Salinity Stress\\n3. Temperature Stress (Heat or Cold)\\n4. Nutrient Deficiency or Toxicity\\n5. Light Stress (Insufficient or Excessive Radiation)\\n6. CO\\u2082 Imbalance\\n7. Hypoxia (Low Root Oxygen)\\nEach stress type affects:\\n* Photosynthesis efficiency\\n* Biomass accumulation\\n* Reproductive development\\n* Nutrient composition\\n* Harvest index\\n---\\n# 4.3 Water Stress\\n## 4.3.1 Drought Stress\\n### Physiological Mechanism\\nWhen water availability decreases:\\n* Stomata close to reduce transpiration\\n* CO\\u2082 intake decreases\\n* Photosynthesis rate declines\\n* Cellular turgor pressure decreases\\n* Growth rate slows\\nProlonged drought leads to:\\n* Reduced leaf expansion\\n* Reduced root and shoot biomass\\n* Lower yield\\n---\\n### Observable Symptoms\\n* Leaf wilting\\n* Reduced leaf size\\n* Leaf curling\\n* Slowed growth rate\\n* Increased root-to-shoot ratio\\n---\\n### Impact on Crops\\n| Crop Type | Sensitivity Level |\\n| - | - |\\n| Lettuce | High |\\n| Radish | Moderate |\\n| Beans/Peas | Moderate |\\n| Potatoes | Moderate to High |\\nLeafy greens are particularly sensitive due to high water content.\\n---\\n### Mitigation in CEA Context\\n* Increase irrigation frequency\\n* Adjust nutrient solution flow rate\\n* Increase humidity to reduce transpiration\\n* Reduce light intensity temporarily to reduce evaporative demand\\n---\\n## 4.3.2 Overwatering / Waterlogging\\n### Mechanism\\nExcess water reduces oxygen availability in the root zone.\\n* Root respiration decreases\\n* Root rot risk increases\\n* Nutrient uptake declines\\n---\\n### Observable Symptoms\\n* Yellowing leaves (chlorosis)\\n* Slowed growth\\n* Root discoloration\\n* Increased fungal risk\\n---\\n### Mitigation\\n* Improve drainage (hydroponic flow adjustment)\\n* Increase root oxygenation (aeration systems)\\n* Reduce irrigation cycle duration\\n---\\n# 4.4 Salinity Stress\\nRelevant if nutrient solution becomes concentrated due to evaporation.\\n## Mechanism\\nHigh salt concentration:\\n* Increases osmotic pressure\\n* Reduces water uptake\\n* Causes ion toxicity (Na\\u207a, Cl\\u207b excess)\\n---\\n## Observable Symptoms\\n* Leaf edge burn\\n* Reduced leaf expansion\\n* Stunted growth\\n---\\n## Impact on Yield\\nCan reduce biomass accumulation by 20\\u201350% depending on severity.\\n---\\n## Mitigation\\n* Dilute nutrient solution\\n* Monitor electrical conductivity (EC)\\n* Implement closed-loop filtration\\n---\\n# 4.5 Temperature Stress\\n## 4.5.1 Heat Stress\\n### Mechanism\\nAbove optimal range:\\n* Protein denaturation\\n* Reduced photosynthetic efficiency\\n* Increased respiration rate\\n* Accelerated flowering (bolting in lettuce)\\n---\\n### Observable Symptoms\\n* Leaf tip burn\\n* Premature flowering\\n* Reduced leaf mass\\n* Lower tuber size (potatoes)\\n---\\n### Critical Thresholds (Approximate)\\n| Crop | Heat Risk Above |\\n| - | - |\\n| Lettuce | 25\\u00b0C |\\n| Radish | 26\\u00b0C |\\n| Beans | 30\\u00b0C |\\n| Potatoes | 25\\u201328\\u00b0C |\\n---\\n### Mitigation\\n* Increase ventilation\\n* Reduce LED intensity\\n* Increase evaporative cooling\\n* Adjust planting density\\n---\\n## 4.5.2 Cold Stress\\n### Mechanism\\nBelow optimal range:\\n* Reduced enzyme activity\\n* Slower metabolism\\n* Delayed growth cycle\\n---\\n### Symptoms\\n* Purple leaf coloration\\n* Reduced root development\\n* Extended time to harvest\\n---\\n### Mitigation\\n* Increase heating\\n* Optimize insulation\\n* Adjust photoperiod\\n---\\n# 4.6 Nutrient Deficiency\\nPrecise nutrient control is critical in hydroponic systems.\\n---\\n## 4.6.1 Nitrogen (N) Deficiency\\n### Symptoms\\n* Yellowing of older leaves\\n* Reduced growth rate\\n* Lower protein content\\n### Impact\\n* Reduced leaf biomass\\n* Reduced nutritional value (protein)\\n### Mitigation\\n* Increase nitrogen concentration in nutrient solution\\n* Monitor nitrate levels\\n---\\n## 4.6.2 Potassium (K) Deficiency\\n### Symptoms\\n* Leaf edge browning\\n* Weak stems\\n* Reduced tuber size (potatoes)\\n### Mitigation\\n* Adjust K concentration\\n* Monitor solution EC\\n---\\n## 4.6.3 Iron (Fe) Deficiency\\n### Symptoms\\n* Yellowing of young leaves (interveinal chlorosis)\\nCommon in high pH systems.\\n### Mitigation\\n* Adjust pH (optimal 5.5\\u20136.5)\\n* Add chelated iron\\n---\\n# 4.7 Light Stress\\n## Insufficient Light\\n### Mechanism\\n* Reduced photosynthesis\\n* Reduced carbohydrate production\\n### Symptoms\\n* Elongated stems (etiolation)\\n* Pale leaves\\n* Lower biomass\\n### Mitigation\\n* Increase LED intensity\\n* Adjust photoperiod duration\\n---\\n## Excessive Light\\n### Mechanism\\n* Photoinhibition\\n* Chlorophyll damage\\n### Symptoms\\n* Leaf bleaching\\n* Burn spots\\n### Mitigation\\n* Reduce intensity\\n* Adjust light spectrum\\n---\\n# 4.8 CO\\u2082 Imbalance\\n## Low CO\\u2082\\n* Reduced photosynthesis\\n* Slower growth\\n## Excess CO\\u2082 (>1500 ppm)\\n* May stress some species\\n* Must remain safe for astronauts\\nOptimal range for most crops:\\n800\\u20131200 ppm\\n---\\n# 4.9 Root Hypoxia\\nLow oxygen in root zone (common in poorly managed hydroponics).\\n### Symptoms\\n* Root browning\\n* Reduced nutrient uptake\\n* Wilting despite adequate moisture\\n### Mitigation\\n* Increase aeration\\n* Improve circulation\\n---\\n# 4.10 AI-Oriented Stress Response Logic\\nFor autonomous systems, stress management follows:\\n1. Detect anomaly (sensor data)\\n2. Match symptoms to probable stressor\\n3. Validate via secondary indicators\\n4. Trigger corrective action\\n5. Monitor recovery trend\\nExample:\\nIF:\\n* Soil moisture \\u2193\\n* Leaf wilting \\u2191\\n* Growth rate \\u2193\\nTHEN:\\n* Diagnose drought stress\\n* Increase irrigation\\n* Temporarily reduce light intensity\\n---\\n# 4.11 Yield Impact Hierarchy\\nNot all stress types equally threaten mission success.\\nHigh-risk stress:\\n* Severe water deficit\\n* Nutrient deficiency (N)\\n* Temperature instability\\nModerate risk:\\n* Light imbalance\\n* CO\\u2082 fluctuation\\nLow risk (short-term):\\n* Minor salinity fluctuation\\nAI agents should prioritize interventions accordingly.\\n\", \"score\": 0.5418767, \"location\": {\"s3Location\": {\"uri\": \"s3://start-hack-documents/04_Plant_Stress_and_Response_Guide.md\"}, \"type\": \"S3\"}, \"metadata\": {\"x-amz-bedrock-kb-source-uri\": \"s3://start-hack-documents/04_Plant_Stress_and_Response_Guide.md\", \"x-amz-bedrock-kb-source-file-modality\": \"TEXT\", \"x-amz-bedrock-kb-data-source-id\": \"OGUYTEJRIW\"}}, {\"content\": \"critical systems Energy constraints require optimization. --- ## 6.4.3 AI Detection Signals * Energy consumption spike * Battery/storage decline * System throttling events * Environmental parameter drift --- ## 6.4.4 Recommended Responses Priority model: 1. Maintain life-critical systems 2. Reduce non-essential energy use 3. Optimize lighting schedules 4. Adjust environmental setpoints Example strategy: * Shorten photoperiod * Reduce LED intensity * Prioritize high-efficiency crops --- ## 6.4.5 Recovery Path Once energy stabilizes: * Gradually restore environmental parameters * Monitor crop response * Validate system stability Abrupt changes should be avoided. --- # 6.5 Scenario: Crop Disease or Pathogen Risk ## 6.5.1 Event Description Although controlled environments reduce pathogen risk, possibilities include: * Fungal growth * Bacterial contamination * Cross-contamination * Humidity imbalances Disease events threaten yield and system stability. --- ## 6.5.2 System Impact * Reduced crop productivity * Potential crop loss * Containment requirements Early detection is critical. --- ## 6.5.3 AI Detection Signals * Leaf discoloration * Unusual growth patterns * Humidity anomalies * Sensor irregularities Multi-signal validation is recommended. --- ## 6.5.4 Recommended Responses Containment strategy: 1. Isolate affected zone 2. Reduce humidity 3. Increase monitoring 4. Remove contaminated material if necessary AI agents should\", \"score\": 0.51503175, \"location\": {\"s3Location\": {\"uri\": \"s3://start-hack-documents/06_Greenhouse_Operational_Scenarios.md\"}, \"type\": \"S3\"}, \"metadata\": {\"x-amz-bedrock-kb-source-uri\": \"s3://start-hack-documents/06_Greenhouse_Operational_Scenarios.md\", \"x-amz-bedrock-kb-source-file-modality\": \"TEXT\", \"x-amz-bedrock-kb-chunk-id\": \"1%3A0%3AdpPbpZwBQg_XFYypi8cS\", \"x-amz-bedrock-kb-data-source-id\": \"OGUYTEJRIW\"}}, {\"content\": \"Isolate affected zone 2. Reduce humidity 3. Increase monitoring 4. Remove contaminated material if necessary AI agents should balance containment with yield preservation. --- ## 6.5.5 Recovery Path * Validate zone health * Monitor adjacent areas * Resume normal operations once stable Preventive hygiene protocols reduce recurrence risk. --- # 6.6 Scenario: Temperature Control Failure ## 6.6.1 Event Description Temperature deviation may occur due to: * HVAC malfunction * Sensor error * External environmental factors Controlled range is essential for plant physiology. --- ## 6.6.2 System Impact Heat stress: * Bolting in lettuce * Reduced yield Cold stress: * Slower metabolism * Growth delays --- ## 6.6.3 AI Detection Signals * Temperature drift * Sensor disagreement * Plant stress indicators --- ## 6.6.4 Recommended Responses Heat scenario: * Increase ventilation * Reduce light intensity * Adjust setpoints Cold scenario: * Activate heating * Optimize insulation * Monitor growth rates --- ## 6.6.5 Recovery Path Gradual return to optimal range prevents shock responses. --- # 6.7 Scenario: CO\\u2082 Imbalance ## 6.7.1 Event Description CO\\u2082 levels may drift due to: * System leakage * Consumption imbalance * Sensor issues Plants require CO\\u2082 for photosynthesis but astronauts require safe air composition. --- ## 6.7.2 System Impact Low CO\\u2082: * Reduced photosynthesis High CO\\u2082 (beyond safety limits): * Human\", \"score\": 0.4749101, \"location\": {\"s3Location\": {\"uri\": \"s3://start-hack-documents/06_Greenhouse_Operational_Scenarios.md\"}, \"type\": \"S3\"}, \"metadata\": {\"x-amz-bedrock-kb-source-uri\": \"s3://start-hack-documents/06_Greenhouse_Operational_Scenarios.md\", \"x-amz-bedrock-kb-source-file-modality\": \"TEXT\", \"x-amz-bedrock-kb-chunk-id\": \"1%3A0%3Ad5PbpZwBQg_XFYypi8cS\", \"x-amz-bedrock-kb-data-source-id\": \"OGUYTEJRIW\"}}], \"total_results\": 3}"}

query: How does crop disease spread between plants? What containment strategies should be used?

Extended Crop Profiles for Martian Controlled Environment Agriculture\\n## 3.1 Purpose\\nThis document provides structured agronomic profiles of selected crops suitable for a Martian greenhouse supporting a crew of four astronauts during a 450-day surface mission.\\nEach crop profile includes:\\n* Growth characteristics\\n* Yield potential\\n* Resource requirements\\n* Nutritional contribution\\n* Environmental sensitivities\\n* Suitability for mission objectives\\nThe goal is to support AI agents in:\\n* Crop selection optimization\\n* Resource allocation decisions\\n* Nutritional balancing\\n* Stress prioritization\\n---\\n# 3.2 Crop Selection Criteria\\nCrops were selected based on:\\n* High edible biomass fraction (harvest index)\\n* Short to moderate growth cycle\\n* High nutrient density\\n* Compatibility with hydroponic systems\\n* Controlled environment adaptability\\nPrimary crop categories:\\n* Leafy Greens\\n* Root/Tuber Crops\\n* Legumes\\n* Culinary Herbs\\n---\\n# 3.3 Leafy Greens \\u2013 Lettuce (Lactuca sativa)\\n## Growth Characteristics\\n* Growth cycle: 30\\u201345 days\\n* Harvest index: ~0.7\\u20130.9 (most biomass edible)\\n* Typical yield (CEA systems): 3\\u20135 kg/m\\u00b2 per cycle\\n* Root depth: shallow\\n* Growth habit: vegetative leaf biomass\\nFast turnover makes lettuce suitable for continuous harvest systems.\\n---\\n## Environmental Requirements\\n* Optimal temperature: 15\\u201322\\u00b0C\\n* Heat stress threshold: >25\\u00b0C (risk of bolting)\\n* Optimal relative humidity: 50\\u201370%\\n* Light requirement: 150\\u2013250 \\u00b5mol/m\\u00b2/s PAR\\n* CO\\u2082 enrichment benefit: strong\\n---\\n## Water & Nutrient Requirements\\n* High water demand (high tissue water content ~95%)\\n* Nitrogen-sensitive crop\\n* Requires balanced N-P-K solution\\n* Optimal nutrient solution pH: 5.5\\u20136.5\\nWater use efficiency improves significantly under hydroponics.\\n---\\n## Nutritional Contribution (per 100g fresh weight)\\n* ~15 kcal\\n* Rich in Vitamin K\\n* Good source of Vitamin A (beta-carotene)\\n* Moderate folate content\\n* Low protein (~1.4 g)\\nRole in mission:\\nMicronutrient density and dietary diversity.\\n---\\n## Sensitivity Profile\\nHigh sensitivity:\\n* Heat stress\\n* Water deficit\\n* Nitrogen deficiency\\nModerate sensitivity:\\n* Salinity\\nLow tolerance to environmental fluctuation.\\n---\\n## Mission Suitability\\nAdvantages:\\n* Rapid harvest cycles\\n* High edible biomass fraction\\n* Micronutrient rich\\nLimitations:\\n* Low caloric density\\n* Low protein contribution\\nBest used as:\\nMicronutrient stabilizer crop in multi-crop system.\\n---\\n# 3.4 Potatoes (Solanum tuberosum)\\n## Growth Characteristics\\n* Growth cycle: 70\\u2013120 days\\n* Harvest index: ~0.75\\n* Typical CEA yield: 4\\u20138 kg/m\\u00b2 per cycle\\n* Growth habit: underground tuber development\\nHigh caloric return per unit area.\\n---\\n## Environmental Requirements\\n* Optimal temperature: 16\\u201320\\u00b0C\\n* Heat stress threshold: >25\\u201328\\u00b0C\\n* Light requirement: 200\\u2013400 \\u00b5mol/m\\u00b2/s\\n* Requires good root-zone aeration\\n---\\n## Water & Nutrient Requirements\\n* Moderate to high water requirement\\n* High potassium demand\\n* Moderate nitrogen demand\\n* Sensitive to waterlogging\\nOptimal nutrient pH: 5.5\\u20136.0\\n---\\n## Nutritional Contribution (per 100g fresh weight)\\n* ~77 kcal\\n* ~17 g carbohydrates\\n* ~2 g protein\\n* Good potassium source\\n* Low fat content\\nRole in mission:\\nPrimary carbohydrate and caloric backbone.\\n---\\n## Sensitivity Profile\\nModerate sensitivity:\\n* Drought\\n* Heat stress\\nHigh sensitivity:\\n* Root hypoxia\\n---\\n## Mission Suitability\\nAdvantages:\\n* High calorie density\\n* High yield per m\\u00b2\\n* Relatively stable storage potential\\nLimitations:\\n* Longer growth cycle\\n* Less micronutrient diversity\\nBest used as:\\nPrimary energy security crop.\\n---\\n# 3.5 Radishes (Raphanus sativus)\\n## Growth Characteristics\\n* Growth cycle: 21\\u201330 days\\n* Harvest index: ~0.6\\u20130.8\\n* Yield: 2\\u20134 kg/m\\u00b2 per cycle\\n* Compact root vegetable\\nVery fast biomass turnover.\\n---\\n## Environmental Requirements\\n* Optimal temperature: 15\\u201322\\u00b0C\\n* Sensitive to inconsistent watering\\n* Light requirement: moderate\\n---\\n## Water & Nutrient Requirements\\n* Consistent moisture required\\n* Moderate nitrogen demand\\n---\\n## Nutritional Contribution (per 100g fresh weight)\\n* ~16 kcal\\n* Vitamin C source\\n* Moderate fiber content\\nRole in mission:\\nDiet diversity and fast yield buffering.\\n---\\n## Sensitivity Profile\\nHigh sensitivity:\\n* Water inconsistency\\nModerate sensitivity:\\n* Heat stress\\n---\\n## Mission Suitability\\nAdvantages:\\n* Very short cycle\\n* Rapid system feedback crop\\nLimitations:\\n* Low caloric density\\nBest used as:\\nShort-cycle stability and variety crop.\\n---\\n# 3.6 Beans & Peas (Legumes)\\nRepresentative crops:\\n* Common bean (Phaseolus vulgaris)\\n* Pea (Pisum sativum)\\n---\\n## Growth Characteristics\\n* Growth cycle: 50\\u201370 days\\n* Harvest index: ~0.5\\u20130.6\\n* Yield: 2\\u20134 kg/m\\u00b2\\nModerate cycle duration.\\n---\\n## Environmental Requirements\\n* Optimal temperature: 18\\u201325\\u00b0C\\n* Sensitive to frost\\n* Moderate light requirement\\n---\\n## Water & Nutrient Requirements\\n* Moderate water need\\n* Nitrogen fixation capability (symbiotic bacteria)\\n* Phosphorus-sensitive\\nIn hydroponics, nitrogen fixation may be reduced and external supply required.\\n---\\n## Nutritional Contribution (per 100g fresh weight)\\n* ~80\\u2013120 kcal\\n* 5\\u20139 g protein\\n* Complex carbohydrates\\n* Fiber-rich\\nRole in mission:\\nPrimary plant-based protein source.\\n---\\n## Sensitivity Profile\\nModerate sensitivity:\\n* Salinity\\n* Water stress\\n---\\n## Mission Suitability\\nAdvantages:\\n* Protein supply\\n* Nutritional diversity\\nLimitations:\\n* Moderate yield compared to potatoes\\n* Longer growth than leafy greens\\nBest used as:\\nProtein security crop.\\n---\\n# 3.7 Culinary Herbs (e.g., Basil, Chives)\\n## Growth Characteristics\\n* Short growth cycle\\n* Low space requirement\\n* Low biomass yield\\n---\\n## Nutritional & Psychological Role\\n* Minimal caloric contribution\\n* Improve palatability\\n* Increase dietary satisfaction\\n* Support crew morale\\n---\\n## Mission Suitability\\nBest used as:\\nPsychological well-being enhancer.\\n---\\n# 3.8 Comparative Crop Matrix\\n| Crop | Cycle Length | Caloric Density | Protein Contribution | Water Demand | System Stability Role |\\n| - | - | - | - | - | - |\\n| Lettuce | Short | Low | Low | High | Micronutrient |\\n| Potato | Long | High | Moderate | Moderate | Energy Backbone |\\n| Radish | Very Short | Low | Low | Moderate | Fast Buffer |\\n| Beans/Peas | Medium | Moderate | High | Moderate | Protein Security |\\n---\\n# 3.9 Strategic Crop Portfolio Logic\\nBalanced mission crop portfolio should:\\n* Ensure caloric security (potatoes)\\n* Ensure protein adequacy (legumes)\\n* Ensure micronutrient sufficiency (leafy greens)\\n* Ensure psychological diversity (herbs)\\n* Maintain short-cycle crop for system resilience (radish/lettuce)\\nAI agents may dynamically adjust planting density and crop ratios based on:\\n* Resource availability\\n* Nutritional deficits\\n* Environmental instability\\n* Energy constraints\\n\", \"score\": 0.42194244, \"location\": {\"s3Location\": {\"uri\": \"s3://start-hack-documents/03_Crop_Profiles_Extended.md\"}, \"type\": \"S3\"}, \"metadata\": {\"x-amz-bedrock-kb-source-uri\": \"s3://start-hack-documents/03_Crop_Profiles_Extended.md\", \"x-amz-bedrock-kb-source-file-modality\": \"TEXT\", \"x-amz-bedrock-kb-data-source-id\": \"OGUYTEJRIW\"}}, {\"content\": \"# 4. Plant Stress and Response Guide for Autonomous Greenhouse Systems\\n## 4.1 Purpose\\nThis document describes major abiotic stressors relevant to a Martian controlled-environment greenhouse, including:\\n* Physiological mechanisms\\n* Observable symptoms\\n* Impact on crop yield and nutritional value\\n* Mitigation strategies within a controlled agriculture system\\nThe objective is to support AI agents in:\\n* Detecting plant stress conditions\\n* Diagnosing probable causes\\n* Triggering corrective environmental or nutritional actions\\n* Optimizing long-term crop stability and yield\\n---\\n# 4.2 Overview of Abiotic Stress in Controlled Environments\\nAbiotic stress refers to negative impacts on plant growth caused by non-living environmental factors.\\nPrimary stress categories relevant to Mars greenhouse systems:\\n1. Water Stress (Drought or Overwatering)\\n2. Salinity Stress\\n3. Temperature Stress (Heat or Cold)\\n4. Nutrient Deficiency or Toxicity\\n5. Light Stress (Insufficient or Excessive Radiation)\\n6. CO\\u2082 Imbalance\\n7. Hypoxia (Low Root Oxygen)\\nEach stress type affects:\\n* Photosynthesis efficiency\\n* Biomass accumulation\\n* Reproductive development\\n* Nutrient composition\\n* Harvest index\\n---\\n# 4.3 Water Stress\\n## 4.3.1 Drought Stress\\n### Physiological Mechanism\\nWhen water availability decreases:\\n* Stomata close to reduce transpiration\\n* CO\\u2082 intake decreases\\n* Photosynthesis rate declines\\n* Cellular turgor pressure decreases\\n* Growth rate slows\\nProlonged drought leads to:\\n* Reduced leaf expansion\\n* Reduced root and shoot biomass\\n* Lower yield\\n---\\n### Observable Symptoms\\n* Leaf wilting\\n* Reduced leaf size\\n* Leaf curling\\n* Slowed growth rate\\n* Increased root-to-shoot ratio\\n---\\n### Impact on Crops\\n| Crop Type | Sensitivity Level |\\n| - | - |\\n| Lettuce | High |\\n| Radish | Moderate |\\n| Beans/Peas | Moderate |\\n| Potatoes | Moderate to High |\\nLeafy greens are particularly sensitive due to high water content.\\n---\\n### Mitigation in CEA Context\\n* Increase irrigation frequency\\n* Adjust nutrient solution flow rate\\n* Increase humidity to reduce transpiration\\n* Reduce light intensity temporarily to reduce evaporative demand\\n---\\n## 4.3.2 Overwatering / Waterlogging\\n### Mechanism\\nExcess water reduces oxygen availability in the root zone.\\n* Root respiration decreases\\n* Root rot risk increases\\n* Nutrient uptake declines\\n---\\n### Observable Symptoms\\n* Yellowing leaves (chlorosis)\\n* Slowed growth\\n* Root discoloration\\n* Increased fungal risk\\n---\\n### Mitigation\\n* Improve drainage (hydroponic flow adjustment)\\n* Increase root oxygenation (aeration systems)\\n* Reduce irrigation cycle duration\\n---\\n# 4.4 Salinity Stress\\nRelevant if nutrient solution becomes concentrated due to evaporation.\\n## Mechanism\\nHigh salt concentration:\\n* Increases osmotic pressure\\n* Reduces water uptake\\n* Causes ion toxicity (Na\\u207a, Cl\\u207b excess)\\n---\\n## Observable Symptoms\\n* Leaf edge burn\\n* Reduced leaf expansion\\n* Stunted growth\\n---\\n## Impact on Yield\\nCan reduce biomass accumulation by 20\\u201350% depending on severity.\\n---\\n## Mitigation\\n* Dilute nutrient solution\\n* Monitor electrical conductivity (EC)\\n* Implement closed-loop filtration\\n---\\n# 4.5 Temperature Stress\\n## 4.5.1 Heat Stress\\n### Mechanism\\nAbove optimal range:\\n* Protein denaturation\\n* Reduced photosynthetic efficiency\\n* Increased respiration rate\\n* Accelerated flowering (bolting in lettuce)\\n---\\n### Observable Symptoms\\n* Leaf tip burn\\n* Premature flowering\\n* Reduced leaf mass\\n* Lower tuber size (potatoes)\\n---\\n### Critical Thresholds (Approximate)\\n| Crop | Heat Risk Above |\\n| - | - |\\n| Lettuce | 25\\u00b0C |\\n| Radish | 26\\u00b0C |\\n| Beans | 30\\u00b0C |\\n| Potatoes | 25\\u201328\\u00b0C |\\n---\\n### Mitigation\\n* Increase ventilation\\n* Reduce LED intensity\\n* Increase evaporative cooling\\n* Adjust planting density\\n---\\n## 4.5.2 Cold Stress\\n### Mechanism\\nBelow optimal range:\\n* Reduced enzyme activity\\n* Slower metabolism\\n* Delayed growth cycle\\n---\\n### Symptoms\\n* Purple leaf coloration\\n* Reduced root development\\n* Extended time to harvest\\n---\\n### Mitigation\\n* Increase heating\\n* Optimize insulation\\n* Adjust photoperiod\\n---\\n# 4.6 Nutrient Deficiency\\nPrecise nutrient control is critical in hydroponic systems.\\n---\\n## 4.6.1 Nitrogen (N) Deficiency\\n### Symptoms\\n* Yellowing of older leaves\\n* Reduced growth rate\\n* Lower protein content\\n### Impact\\n* Reduced leaf biomass\\n* Reduced nutritional value (protein)\\n### Mitigation\\n* Increase nitrogen concentration in nutrient solution\\n* Monitor nitrate levels\\n---\\n## 4.6.2 Potassium (K) Deficiency\\n### Symptoms\\n* Leaf edge browning\\n* Weak stems\\n* Reduced tuber size (potatoes)\\n### Mitigation\\n* Adjust K concentration\\n* Monitor solution EC\\n---\\n## 4.6.3 Iron (Fe) Deficiency\\n### Symptoms\\n* Yellowing of young leaves (interveinal chlorosis)\\nCommon in high pH systems.\\n### Mitigation\\n* Adjust pH (optimal 5.5\\u20136.5)\\n* Add chelated iron\\n---\\n# 4.7 Light Stress\\n## Insufficient Light\\n### Mechanism\\n* Reduced photosynthesis\\n* Reduced carbohydrate production\\n### Symptoms\\n* Elongated stems (etiolation)\\n* Pale leaves\\n* Lower biomass\\n### Mitigation\\n* Increase LED intensity\\n* Adjust photoperiod duration\\n---\\n## Excessive Light\\n### Mechanism\\n* Photoinhibition\\n* Chlorophyll damage\\n### Symptoms\\n* Leaf bleaching\\n* Burn spots\\n### Mitigation\\n* Reduce intensity\\n* Adjust light spectrum\\n---\\n# 4.8 CO\\u2082 Imbalance\\n## Low CO\\u2082\\n* Reduced photosynthesis\\n* Slower growth\\n## Excess CO\\u2082 (>1500 ppm)\\n* May stress some species\\n* Must remain safe for astronauts\\nOptimal range for most crops:\\n800\\u20131200 ppm\\n---\\n# 4.9 Root Hypoxia\\nLow oxygen in root zone (common in poorly managed hydroponics).\\n### Symptoms\\n* Root browning\\n* Reduced nutrient uptake\\n* Wilting despite adequate moisture\\n### Mitigation\\n* Increase aeration\\n* Improve circulation\\n---\\n# 4.10 AI-Oriented Stress Response Logic\\nFor autonomous systems, stress management follows:\\n1. Detect anomaly (sensor data)\\n2. Match symptoms to probable stressor\\n3. Validate via secondary indicators\\n4. Trigger corrective action\\n5. Monitor recovery trend\\nExample:\\nIF:\\n* Soil moisture \\u2193\\n* Leaf wilting \\u2191\\n* Growth rate \\u2193\\nTHEN:\\n* Diagnose drought stress\\n* Increase irrigation\\n* Temporarily reduce light intensity\\n---\\n# 4.11 Yield Impact Hierarchy\\nNot all stress types equally threaten mission success.\\nHigh-risk stress:\\n* Severe water deficit\\n* Nutrient deficiency (N)\\n* Temperature instability\\nModerate risk:\\n* Light imbalance\\n* CO\\u2082 fluctuation\\nLow risk (short-term):\\n* Minor salinity fluctuation\\nAI agents should prioritize interventions accordingly.\\n\", \"score\": 0.41314286, \"location\": {\"s3Location\": {\"uri\": \"s3://start-hack-documents/04_Plant_Stress_and_Response_Guide.md\"}, \"type\": \"S3\"}, \"metadata\": {\"x-amz-bedrock-kb-source-uri\": \"s3://start-hack-documents/04_Plant_Stress_and_Response_Guide.md\", \"x-amz-bedrock-kb-source-file-modality\": \"TEXT\", \"x-amz-bedrock-kb-data-source-id\": \"OGUYTEJRIW\"}}, {\"content\": \"Isolate affected zone 2. Reduce humidity 3. Increase monitoring 4. Remove contaminated material if necessary AI agents should balance containment with yield preservation. --- ## 6.5.5 Recovery Path * Validate zone health * Monitor adjacent areas * Resume normal operations once stable Preventive hygiene protocols reduce recurrence risk. --- # 6.6 Scenario: Temperature Control Failure ## 6.6.1 Event Description Temperature deviation may occur due to: * HVAC malfunction * Sensor error * External environmental factors Controlled range is essential for plant physiology. --- ## 6.6.2 System Impact Heat stress: * Bolting in lettuce * Reduced yield Cold stress: * Slower metabolism * Growth delays --- ## 6.6.3 AI Detection Signals * Temperature drift * Sensor disagreement * Plant stress indicators --- ## 6.6.4 Recommended Responses Heat scenario: * Increase ventilation * Reduce light intensity * Adjust setpoints Cold scenario: * Activate heating * Optimize insulation * Monitor growth rates --- ## 6.6.5 Recovery Path Gradual return to optimal range prevents shock responses. --- # 6.7 Scenario: CO\\u2082 Imbalance ## 6.7.1 Event Description CO\\u2082 levels may drift due to: * System leakage * Consumption imbalance * Sensor issues Plants require CO\\u2082 for photosynthesis but astronauts require safe air composition. --- ## 6.7.2 System Impact Low CO\\u2082: * Reduced photosynthesis High CO\\u2082 (beyond safety limits): * Human\", \"score\": 0.41312662, \"location\": {\"s3Location\": {\"uri\": \"s3://start-hack-documents/06_Greenhouse_Operational_Scenarios.md\"}, \"type\": \"S3\"}, \"metadata\": {\"x-amz-bedrock-kb-source-uri\": \"s3://start-hack-documents/06_Greenhouse_Operational_Scenarios.md\", \"x-amz-bedrock-kb-source-file-modality\": \"TEXT\", \"x-amz-bedrock-kb-chunk-id\": \"1%3A0%3Ad5PbpZwBQg_XFYypi8cS\", \"x-amz-bedrock-kb-data-source-id\": \"OGUYTEJRIW\"}}, {\"content\": \"# 7. Innovation Impact: Translating Martian Greenhouse Technology to Earth Agriculture\\n## 7.1 Purpose\\nThe technologies and systems developed for autonomous Martian agriculture are not limited to space applications.\\nMany of the challenges addressed by:\\n* Closed-loop resource management\\n* Autonomous decision systems\\n* Controlled environment agriculture\\n* Nutrient optimization\\nhave direct relevance to Earth-based agriculture and sustainability.\\nThis document connects hackathon innovation to real-world impact.\\n---\\n## 7.2 Innovation Transfer Principles\\nTechnologies designed for extreme environments often generate:\\n\\u2714 Efficiency improvements\\n\\u2714 Resource optimization\\n\\u2714 New decision paradigms\\n\\u2714 Sustainability benefits\\nThe Martian greenhouse challenge functions as:\\n> A testbed for next-generation agricultural intelligence.\\n---\\n## 7.3 Earth Agricultural Challenges\\nGlobal agriculture faces persistent constraints:\\n* Water scarcity\\n* Soil degradation\\n* Climate variability\\n* Resource inefficiency\\n* Labor shortages\\nAutonomous and precision systems can mitigate these pressures.\\n---\\n### 7.3.1 Water Scarcity\\nFreshwater resources are unevenly distributed.\\nImpact:\\n* Reduced crop yields\\n* Increased production risk\\n* Regional food insecurity\\nClosed-loop water principles from controlled environments can inform:\\n* Efficient irrigation\\n* Water recycling\\n* Precision usage\\n---\\n### 7.3.2 Soil Degradation\\nIntensive agriculture can reduce soil quality through:\\n* Nutrient depletion\\n* Erosion\\n* Organic matter loss\\nLessons from soilless and hydroponic systems include:\\n* Nutrient precision\\n* Reduced dependency on soil health\\n* Alternative cultivation models\\n---\\n### 7.3.3 Climate Variability\\nIncreasing climate volatility affects:\\n* Crop predictability\\n* Growing seasons\\n* Regional productivity\\nControlled environment methods provide:\\n* Stable production systems\\n* Reduced environmental dependency\\n* Predictable yields\\n---\\n### 7.3.4 Resource Efficiency\\nTraditional agriculture often involves:\\n* Water loss\\n* Fertilizer runoff\\n* Inefficient energy use\\nAutonomous optimization can improve:\\n* Input-output ratios\\n* Environmental impact\\n* Operational efficiency\\n---\\n### 7.3.5 Labor Constraints\\nMany agricultural regions face:\\n* Workforce shortages\\n* Aging labor populations\\n* Operational dependency on manual processes\\nAutonomous systems can:\\n* Reduce labor intensity\\n* Increase operational scalability\\n* Enable remote monitoring\\n---\\n## 7.4 Technological Parallels\\nThe following parallels illustrate innovation transfer:\\n| Mars System | Earth Application |\\n| - | - |\\n| Closed-loop water | Precision irrigation |\\n| Autonomous agents | Farm decision systems |\\n| Environmental control | Protected cultivation |\\n| Nutrient optimization | Fertilizer efficiency |\\n| Multi-agent reasoning | Farm intelligence |\\nThese parallels demonstrate real-world relevance.\\n---\\n## 7.5 Sustainability Benefits\\nAutonomous and precision systems support:\\n\\u2714 Reduced resource consumption\\n\\u2714 Lower environmental impact\\n\\u2714 Efficient nutrient use\\n\\u2714 Sustainable production\\nSustainability is both a mission requirement and a global imperative.\\n---\\n## 7.6 Role of AI and Multi-Agent Systems\\nAI systems developed for greenhouse autonomy can:\\n* Interpret sensor data\\n* Optimize decisions\\n* Detect anomalies\\n* Adapt strategies\\nSimilar architectures can support Earth agriculture at scale.\\n---\\n## 7.7 Educational and Innovation Value\\nHackathons provide:\\n\\u2714 Experiential learning\\n\\u2714 Systems thinking\\n\\u2714 Collaborative innovation\\n\\u2714 Real-world problem solving\\nParticipants develop skills transferable to industry and research.\\n---\\n## 7.8 Strategic Perspective\\nTechnological development for extreme environments often yields:\\n> Unexpected and valuable Earth applications.\\nSpace exploration and agricultural innovation share common principles:\\n* Efficiency\\n* Resilience\\n* Adaptability\\n* Scientific rigor\\n---\\n## 7.9 Ethical and Sustainability Considerations\\nInnovation must align with:\\n\\u2714 Environmental stewardship\\n\\u2714 Human well-being\\n\\u2714 Responsible development\\nTechnology serves society when applied thoughtfully.\\n---\\n## 7.10 Conclusion\\nThe Martian greenhouse challenge is more than a simulation.\\nIt represents:\\n* A platform for innovation\\n* A test of autonomous intelligence\\n* A pathway to sustainable solutions\\nThe skills and ideas developed may contribute to:\\n> Better agriculture on Earth and beyond.\\n\", \"score\": 0.40493828, \"location\": {\"s3Location\": {\"uri\": \"s3://start-hack-documents/07_From_Mars_to_Earth_Innovation_Impact.md\"}, \"type\": \"S3\"}, \"metadata\": {\"x-amz-bedrock-kb-source-uri\": \"s3://start-hack-documents/07_From_Mars_to_Earth_Innovation_Impact.md\", \"x-amz-bedrock-kb-source-file-modality\": \"TEXT\",
